chat_summary: Git is a distributed version control system. Here's how its core concepts connect.

cards.heading: Commit
cards.body: A snapshot of your entire project at a point in time. Every commit has a unique SHA hash and points to its parent commit, forming a chain.

cards.heading: Tree & Blob
cards.body: A **tree** is a directory listing. A **blob** is a file's content. Commits point to trees, trees point to blobs. Git stores everything by content hash.

cards.heading: Working Directory
cards.body: The files you actually edit on disk. Changes here are *unstaged* — Git knows about them but won't include them in the next commit.

cards.heading: Staging Area (Index)
cards.body: A middle layer between your working directory and the repo. `git add` moves changes here. Only staged changes go into the next commit.

cards.heading: Local Repository
cards.body: The `.git` folder on your machine. Contains the full history, all objects, and all branches. `git commit` writes to here.

cards.heading: Remote (origin)
cards.body: A copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (GitHub, GitLab). `git push` sends your commits there. `git pull` fetches and merges their commits into yours.

cards.heading: Branch
cards.body: A lightweight pointer to a commit. Creating a branch is instant — it's just a file containing a SHA. `HEAD` points to whichever branch you're on.
